content:
{
  "title": "Counterfactual Contrastive Prompting for Bias Mitigation in Large Language Models",
  "problem": "Large language models (LLMs) consistently manifest social biases and stereotypes in generated outputs, even when prompted with instructions to be neutral. Existing bias mitigation techniques often require model fine-tuning or post-processing, which are not always feasible for API-based models.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs are deployed in high-stakes, real-world applications, reducing the propagation of social biases is critical for fairness, trust, and inclusivity. Prompt-based bias mitigation is especially urgent as it can be universally applied across closed-source or commercial models without access to their weights or architectures.",
  "method": "We propose Counterfactual Contrastive Prompting (CCP): for any input prompt, we automatically generate minimally-different counterfactual versions by systematically substituting protected attributes (e.g., gender, ethnicity) and append a contrastive instruction that compels the model to generate identical or equivalent outputs across all counterfactuals. The final prompt takes the form: 'Given the following variations of a scenario that differ only in [protected attribute], provide a response that is invariant to these differences.' The method leverages prompt chaining and in-context demonstrations to enforce intra-prompt consistency, discouraging the model from reflecting or amplifying social stereotypes.",
  "experiment_plan": "We evaluate CCP on standardized bias benchmarks such as StereoSet and CrowS-Pairs, comparing against direct prompting and existing bias-mitigation prompt techniques. Metrics include stereotype score reduction, language generation quality (BLEU/ROUGE), and human evaluation for perceived impartiality. We also analyze zero-shot generalization to unseen bias domains using newly collected prompts."
}
